keyPoints: Serious illnesses like renal, hepatic, or cardiac failure are common causes of hypoglycemia in hospitalized patients.
Sepsis leads to increased glucose utilization due to cytokine production.
Starvation results in hypoglycemia due to alternative substrate utilization and glycogen depletion.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Endogenous Hyperinsulinism
items.content: Endogenous hyperinsulinism is characterized by inappropriate insulin secretion during hypoglycemia. Key diagnostic criteria include specific plasma concentrations of insulin, C-peptide, and proinsulin when glucose levels are low.
keyPoints: Plasma insulin concentration ≥3 μU/mL (≥18 pmol/L)
Plasma C-peptide concentration ≥0.6 ng/mL (≥0.2 nmol/L)
Plasma proinsulin concentration ≥5.0 pmol/L
Plasma glucose concentration <55 mg/dL (<3.0 mmol/L) with symptoms of hypoglycemia
Low plasma β-hydroxybutyrate concentration (≤2.7 mmol/L)
Increment in plasma glucose level >25 mg/dL (>1.4 mmol/L) after IV glucagon administration
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Strategy for Hypoglycemia
items.content: The diagnostic approach involves measuring key plasma concentrations during hypoglycemia and confirming Whipple's triad to establish the presence of endogenous hyperinsulinism.
keyPoints: Measure plasma glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, and β-hydroxybutyrate
Screen for circulating oral hypoglycemic agents during hypoglycemia
Document Whipple's triad: symptoms of hypoglycemia, low plasma glucose, and resolution with glucose or glucagon
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 2)

items.title: Management of Endogenous Hyperinsulinism
items.content: Treatment options for endogenous hyperinsulinism vary based on the underlying cause, ranging from medical therapy to surgical intervention.
keyPoints: Insulinoma is a common cause and may require curative surgery
Medical treatments include α-glucosidase inhibitors, diazoxide, or octreotide
Partial pancreatectomy may be necessary if medical treatment fails
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 2)

items.subtopic: HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 3) | ACCIDENTAL, SURREPTITIOUS, OR MALICIOUS HYPOGLYCEMIA
items.title: Insulinoma Characteristics and Detection
items.content: Insulinomas are a treatable cause of hypoglycemia with >90% being benign. They typically present in the third decade if associated with multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1, but have a median age at presentation of 50 years otherwise. Most insulinomas are small (<2.0 cm) and located within the pancreas. Imaging techniques such as CT or MRI detect ~70–80% of cases, while endoscopic ultrasound has higher sensitivity (~90%).
keyPoints: Incidence: 1 in 250,000 per year.
>90% are benign.
Median age at presentation: 50 years (sporadic), third decade with MEN1.
Tumor size: Usually <2.0 cm.
Location: >99% within the pancreas.
Imaging detection: CT/MRI ~70–80%, endoscopic ultrasound ~90%.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 3) | ACCIDENTAL, SURREPTITIOUS, OR MALICIOUS HYPOGLYCEMIA

items.subtopic: HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 3) | ACCIDENTAL, SURREPTITIOUS, OR MALICIOUS HYPOGLYCEMIA
items.title: Differentiating Insulinoma from Factitious Hypoglycemia
items.content: Factitious hypoglycemia can mimic insulinoma but is distinguished by low C-peptide levels due to exogenous insulin administration. Accidental ingestion or surreptitious use of insulin secretagogues, common among healthcare workers and diabetics, also presents similarly. Analytical errors in glucose measurement are rare, but hand-held monitors should not be used for definitive diagnosis.
keyPoints: Factitious hypoglycemia: Low C-peptide levels with exogenous insulin.
Accidental ingestion: Increased C-peptide with secretagogue use.
Common among healthcare workers and diabetics.
Analytical error in glucose measurement is rare.
Hand-held monitors are not definitive for diagnosing hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · HYPOGLYCEMIA WITHOUT DIABETES (part 3) | ACCIDENTAL, SURREPTITIOUS, OR MALICIOUS HYPOGLYCEMIA

items.subtopic: INBORN ERRORS OF METABOLISM CAUSING HYPOGLYCEMIA | Hypoglycemia | RECOGNITION AND DOCUMENTATION
items.title: Recognizing and Documenting Hypoglycemia in Inborn Errors of Metabolism
items.content: Hypoglycemia due to inborn errors of metabolism can occur at various times, such as fasting, postprandial, or exercise-induced. Recognition involves identifying typical symptoms during a symptomatic episode and fulfilling Whipple's triad for documentation. This includes measuring plasma glucose levels before administering glucose, confirming low glucose with symptom resolution upon raising the level.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemia can occur in infancy or adulthood due to metabolic errors.
Fasting hypoglycemia may involve disorders like glycogen storage disease (GSD) types 0, I, III, and IV, with high blood lactate levels before meals for GSD type I and after meals for GSD type III.
Postprandial hypoglycemia can result from mutations in glucokinase, SUR1, Kir6.2 potassium channels, congenital disorders of glycosylation, or inherited fructose intolerance.
Exercise-induced hypoglycemia is characterized by hyperinsulinemia due to increased activity of monocarboxylate transporter 1 in beta cells.
Whipple's triad for hypoglycemia documentation: symptomatic episode with low plasma glucose that resolves upon raising the level.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · INBORN ERRORS OF METABOLISM CAUSING HYPOGLYCEMIA | Hypoglycemia | RECOGNITION AND DOCUMENTATION

items.subtopic: INBORN ERRORS OF METABOLISM CAUSING HYPOGLYCEMIA | Hypoglycemia | RECOGNITION AND DOCUMENTATION
items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Suspected Inborn Metabolic Errors Causing Hypoglycemia
items.content: When hypoglycemia is suspected due to inborn errors, a thorough diagnostic approach includes measuring plasma glucose during symptomatic episodes and assessing Whipple's triad. Additional tests should include plasma insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate levels, screening for oral hypoglycemic agents, and symptom assessment before and after raising glucose levels.
keyPoints: Suspect hypoglycemia in patients with typical symptoms or altered consciousness during known clinical settings.
Measure plasma glucose before administering glucose to document low concentration.
Confirm hypoglycemia by fulfilling Whipple's triad: symptomatic episode, low plasma glucose, and symptom resolution upon raising glucose.
Additional tests for obscure causes include plasma insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate levels, and screening for oral hypoglycemic agents.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · INBORN ERRORS OF METABOLISM CAUSING HYPOGLYCEMIA | Hypoglycemia | RECOGNITION AND DOCUMENTATION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF THE HYPOGLYCEMIC MECHANISM | URGENT TREATMENT | PREVENTION OF RECURRENT HYPOGLYCEMIA
items.title: Diagnosing Hypoglycemic Mechanisms and Initial Management
items.content: In patients with documented hypoglycemia, the mechanism can often be deduced from history, physical examination, and lab data. Drugs are a primary consideration for etiology, even without known use. If no clear cause is identified in an otherwise healthy individual, endogenous hyper-insulinism should be considered. For urgent treatment, oral glucose (15-20 g) is recommended if the patient can take it; otherwise, IV glucose (25 g) or glucagon (1.0 mg for adults) may be used.
keyPoints: Drugs are a primary consideration in hypoglycemia diagnosis.
Oral glucose treatment: 15-20 g is recommended if the patient can take it.
IV glucose administration should start with 25 g, followed by a guided infusion.
Glucagon (1.0 mg for adults) is used when IV therapy isn't practical.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · DIAGNOSIS OF THE HYPOGLYCEMIC MECHANISM | URGENT TREATMENT | PREVENTION OF RECURRENT HYPOGLYCEMIA

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF THE HYPOGLYCEMIC MECHANISM | URGENT TREATMENT | PREVENTION OF RECURRENT HYPOGLYCEMIA
items.title: Preventing Recurrent Hypoglycemia: Understanding Mechanisms and Treatment Strategies
items.content: To prevent recurrent hypoglycemia, identify the underlying mechanism. Discontinue or reduce offending drugs; sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia can last hours to days. Treat critical illnesses, replace deficient hormones like cortisol or growth hormone, and consider surgical interventions for tumors causing hypoglycemia. For insulinomas, surgery is curative; diazoxide or octreotide may be used if resection isn't possible. In autoimmune cases, glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants might help. Frequent feedings and uncooked cornstarch at bedtime can manage persistent episodes.
keyPoints: Identify and address the underlying hypoglycemic mechanism for prevention.
Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia may persist for hours to days.
Cortisol or growth hormone replacement is necessary if deficient.
Insulinoma treatment: surgical resection is curative; diazoxide/octreotide as alternatives.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia · DIAGNOSIS OF THE HYPOGLYCEMIC MECHANISM | URGENT TREATMENT | PREVENTION OF RECURRENT HYPOGLYCEMIA
